children.name: Cathartes
children.description: A genus of three medium-sized vultures with highly developed olfactory senses, a rare adaptation among raptors that allows them to locate carrion by scent. Their naked heads and dark plumage are classic vulture traits, and they are among the most efficient scavengers in the Americas.

children.name: Cathartes aura
children.description: The most widespread New World vulture, ranging from southern Canada to the tip of South America. Its distinctive red, unfeathered head resembles that of a wild turkey, and it soars with wings held in a pronounced dihedral, rocking unsteadily as it follows its nose to carrion.

children.name: Cathartes burrovianus
children.description: A lowland vulture of marshes and wet savannas from Mexico to northern Argentina. Its bare head is yellow-orange with a blue-grey crown, and it flies lower than the turkey vulture, scanning for carrion with exceptional olfactory sensitivity.

children.name: Cathartes melambrotus
children.description: A large South American vulture of the Amazon rainforest, distinguished from the lesser yellow-headed vulture by its larger size, stouter bill, and more uniformly dark plumage with a deeper yellow-orange head.

children.name: Coragyps
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the black vulture, a highly social and aggressive scavenger that often displaces other vultures at carcasses. Lacking a well-developed sense of smell, it relies on sight and follows turkey vultures to locate food.

children.name: Coragyps atratus
children.description: A stocky, black-plumaged vulture with a bare grey-black head and white patches at the wingtips, visible in flight. Highly gregarious, it roosts and forages in large flocks, and its bold behaviour brings it regularly to urban areas and roadsides throughout the Americas.

children.name: Gymnogyps
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the California condor, one of the world's rarest and most celebrated birds. With a wingspan exceeding three metres, it is one of the largest flying birds and the subject of an intensive captive-breeding and reintroduction programme that saved it from the brink of extinction.

children.name: Gymnogyps californianus
children.description: A massive black vulture with a bald orange-yellow head and a white triangle under each wing, soaring for hours on thermals. Reduced to just 27 wild individuals in 1987, it was saved through an unprecedented captive-breeding effort that has grown the population to over 500 birds.

children.name: Vultur
children.description: A monotypic Andean genus containing the largest raptor in the world by wingspan. The Andean condor is a cultural icon across South America, representing power and freedom in Andean mythology and featuring prominently on the national coats of arms of several countries.

children.name: Vultur gryphus
children.description: The heaviest flying bird in the world, with a wingspan exceeding 3.3 metres, capable of soaring on thermal updrafts for hours without a single wingbeat. Males are distinguished by a fleshy caruncle on the forehead and a prominent white ruff around the neck.

children.name: Sarcoramphus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the king vulture, the most colourful New World vulture with a brilliantly variegated head in shades of red, orange, yellow, and purple. Its powerful bill allows it to tear through tough hides that smaller vultures cannot penetrate.

children.name: Sarcoramphus papa
children.description: A strikingly colourful scavenger with a multicoloured bare head and a bright orange fleshy caruncle above the bill. Dominant at carcasses, it uses its powerful beak to open large carcasses, providing access for smaller vultures that follow its lead.
